sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Physio documentation should make the assessment, safety decisions, treatment rationale, function, and follow-up plan easy for another clinician to understand and defend.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Initial assessment notes
- Follow-up reviews
- Work capacity reviews
- Telehealth reviews
- Escalation or shared-care handover notes

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Documentation should support continuity, legal defensibility, and clinical governance
- Missing function, red flags, or escalation advice can create risk even if the treatment itself was reasonable
- Notes should reflect what was done and decided, not just what was discussed

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Record presenting problem, red flags screened, baseline function, observed function, treatment provided, and immediate response
- Include safety advice, restrictions, and follow-up timing
- Document escalation if the presentation was outside routine conservative care

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Add work-relevant details such as lifting, walking, driving, stairs, kneeling, and shift tolerance when applicable
- Record consent, chaperone issues where relevant, telehealth limitations, and any collateral communication
- When referring onward, document who received handover and what action was agreed

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Strong physio notes read like a safe clinical story, not just a list of techniques
